original_abstract: The electrochemical reduction of CO into value-added liquid fuels and chemicals represents a sustainable pathway for carbon recycling, yet achieving high activity at low overpotentials remains a significant challenge. Oxide-derived Cu (OD-Cu) has emerged as an exceptionally active catalyst for this process, though the specific nature of its active sites has remained unclear. In this study, we utilize temperature-programmed desorption (TPD) of CO and electron nanodiffraction to investigate the surface chemistry and microstructure of OD-Cu. We identify a unique high-temperature TPD feature on OD-Cu that is absent in conventional polycrystalline Cu foils, indicating the presence of surface sites with a significantly higher binding affinity for CO. To evaluate the role of these sites, we modulated the catalyst’s properties through thermal annealing and by varying precursor oxidation conditions. Our results show that higher annealing temperatures lead to a dramatic decrease—up to 44-fold—in the specific current density for CO reduction, which correlates directly with a loss of these strong-binding surface sites and a reduction in bulk grain boundary density. Based on these findings, we propose that metastable surface defects associated with grain boundary terminations are the primary active sites responsible for the high selectivity and efficiency of OD-Cu toward C2 products. This work establishes a link between microstructural defects and adsorbate binding thermodynamics, providing a critical foundation for the design of next-generation CO2 and CO reduction electrocatalysts.
humanized_abstract: The electrochemical reduction of CO into value-added liquid fuels and chemicals is considered to be one of the most promising carbon recycling strategies. However, efficient catalysts that exhibit high activity at low overpotentials remain to be developed. Recently, oxide-derived Cu (OD-Cu) was confirmed to be an exceptionally active catalyst for CO reduction reaction (CORR). Unfortunately, the active sites that account for such high activity of OD-Cu are not clearly identified. To address this issue, a temperature-programmed desorption (TPD) experiment of CO as well as electron nanodiffraction (END) were employed to study the surface chemistry and structure of OD-Cu. A distinct peak was observed at high temperature (up to 573 K) on OD-Cu catalysts in TPD experiments, which is totally absent on the polycrystalline Cu foils. Moreover, the existence of this feature was also confirmed to be largely dependent on Cu oxide precursors and its thermal annealing conditions. As shown in Fig. 7, when Cu oxide precursors are varied by increasing annealing temperature from 300 to 400 to 500 °C, the activity on OD-Cu catalysts was found to decrease sharply with increases of 28, 36 and up to 44 times, which are attributed to not only reduced number of GB terminations but also reduction in adsorption strength of CO. Therefore, our results showed that metastable surface defects associated with grain boundary terminations are the key active sites for such high selectivity and activity toward C2 products at OD-Cu catalysts.

A distinct peak was observed at high temperature (up to 573 K) on OD-Cu catalysts in TPD experiments, which is totally absent on the polycrystalline Cu foils.
